question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the refillGreedy behavior of rateLimitBucket—which adds tokens as fast as possible rather than uniformly—to send staking reward precompile calls in tight bursts aligned with the refill schedule, achieving an effective throughput higher than the nominal 500 req/sec limit when measured over sub-second windows?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the restore() mechanism—where gasLimitBucket.addTokens() is called after a failed staking reward precompile call—to create a feedback loop: send a call that fails, get gas restored, immediately send another call, repeat indefinitely, effectively making the gas bucket self-replenishing for failed staking reward queries?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket.addTokens() in restore() can add tokens beyond the bucket's capacity (bucket4j addTokens behavior), causing the bucket to overflow and temporarily hold more tokens than gasPerSecond, allowing a super-burst of staking reward precompile calls that exceeds the configured gas-per-second limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of authentication or identity binding in rateLimitBucket to rotate through multiple source IPs or use HTTP/2 multiplexing to submit staking reward precompile calls that all share the same global bucket, effectively making the rate limit a shared resource that can be monopolized to enumerate pending_reward values?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user send a staking reward precompile call with gas exactly equal to GAS_SCALE_FACTOR=10,000 so that scaleGas(10000) returns 0 (since 10000 <= GAS_SCALE_FACTOR), bypassing the gas throttle check entirely, and use this to make unlimited calls to read entity_stake.pending_reward for any account ID without consuming any gas bucket tokens?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user use the opcodeRateLimitBucket endpoint to trace a staking reward precompile call and observe the exact memory layout and storage slots used to store pending_reward and stake_total_start, then use that knowledge to craft optimized direct storage-read calls that bypass higher-level reward access controls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the gasLimitBucket is initialized with capacity equal to getGasPerSecond() (which applies GAS_SCALE_FACTOR scaling) to send a single staking reward precompile call with gas=gasPerSecond (7,500,000,000) that consumes the entire bucket capacity in one request, then wait for the 1-second refill and repeat, creating a monopoly on the gas budget for staking reward queries?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket is a Spring singleton bean shared across all requests to perform a timing attack: by monitoring when the bucket is full (start of each second), the attacker can synchronize staking reward precompile calls to always hit the full bucket, maximizing throughput for pending_reward enumeration?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the interaction between rateLimitBucket (checked first) and gasLimitBucket (checked second) in ThrottleManagerImpl.throttle() to craft requests that pass the rate check but fail the gas check, triggering the gas restore path without actually executing the staking reward precompile, and use this to probe the gas bucket state without consuming rate limit tokens?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential check order in ThrottleManagerImpl (rateLimitBucket checked before gasLimitBucket) to first exhaust the rateLimitBucket with cheap calls, then when the rate bucket refills, immediately send a high-gas staking reward precompile call that consumes the entire gasLimitBucket, effectively using the rate bucket as a timing mechanism to synchronize gas-bucket monopolization?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket has no synchronization strategy to perform a concurrent burst attack where multiple threads simultaneously call tryConsume(1) on a bucket with exactly 1 token remaining, with multiple threads seeing the token as available due to the non-synchronized read, allowing more than 1 additional staking reward precompile call to slip through?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Math.floorDiv behavior in scaleGas() for negative gas values (if gas parameter is not validated to be non-negative) to produce a negative scaled gas value, causing gasLimitBucket.tryConsume() to behave unexpectedly and potentially always return true, bypassing gas throttling for staking reward precompile calls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the opcodeRateLimitBucket (1 req/sec, no synchronization) to race two concurrent opcode trace requests for staking reward precompile calls through the single-token bucket simultaneously, obtaining two traces per second instead of one and doubling the rate of reward state intelligence gathering?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket uses SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED while rateLimitBucket does not, creating an asymmetric locking scenario where the attacker can hold the gasLimitBucket lock with a slow staking reward precompile call while simultaneously racing through the unsynchronized rateLimitBucket with many other calls, creating a priority inversion that delays legitimate reward queries?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the global shared rateLimitBucket to perform a staking reward enumeration attack by sending exactly requestsPerSecond=500 calls per second continuously, each targeting a different account ID to read pending_reward, building a complete database of all accounts with unclaimed staking rewards over time without ever triggering a ThrottleException?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the restore() function's call to gasLimitBucket.addTokens() after a staking reward precompile call that reverts, combined with the fact that addTokens() in bucket4j does not cap at capacity by default, to inflate the gasLimitBucket beyond its configured capacity and enable a super-burst of subsequent staking reward calls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the interaction between gasLimitRefundPercent=100 and the restore() logic to craft a staking reward precompile call that uses minimal gas (e.g., reads pending_reward and immediately returns) but declares a large gasLimit, causing Math.min(gasRemaining, gasLimitToRestoreBaseline) to restore nearly the full gasLimit to gasLimitBucket, making each call effectively free in throttle cost?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of request-level authentication in the rateLimitBucket check to use the web3 API as an oracle for staking reward state, making 500 calls/second to the staking reward precompile with different account IDs and building a real-time map of which accounts have the highest pending_reward values to target for social engineering or front-running attacks?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket capacity is set to getGasPerSecond() which applies GAS_SCALE_FACTOR=10,000 scaling, meaning the actual bucket capacity is gasPerSecond/10000=750,000 tokens, and send 750,000 minimal-gas staking reward precompile calls in a single burst before the bucket empties, enumerating 750,000 accounts' pending_reward values in under one second?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user use the opcodeRateLimitBucket endpoint to trace a staking reward precompile call at the exact moment a staking period ends, observing the EVM state transition that updates pending_reward values, and use that timing information to front-run legitimate reward claims on the actual Hedera network?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the gasLimitBucket refill period is exactly Duration.ofSeconds(1) to synchronize staking reward precompile calls with the refill clock, sending a burst of calls at t=0, t=1, t=2, etc., each time consuming the full bucket capacity and enumerating the maximum number of pending_reward values per second?
